questions.question: Compare adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations in terms of space complexity and suitable graph types.
keyPoints: Adjacency matrix: O(V²) space, suitable for dense graphs
Adjacency list: O(V+E) space, suitable for sparse graphs
Matrix: O(1) edge lookup, List: O(degree) edge lookup

questions.question: Explain the difference between BFS and DFS traversal with respect to data structure used and output order.
keyPoints: BFS uses queue, explores level by level
DFS uses stack or recursion, explores depth-first
BFS produces vertices in order of distance from source
DFS produces vertices in depth-first order

questions.question: Describe the elementary operations for inserting a vertex and an edge in a graph represented as adjacency list.
keyPoints: Insert vertex: Add new head pointer to the array/list of vertices
Insert edge: Create new node, insert at head of adjacency list for source vertex
For undirected: insert in both adjacency lists

questions.question: What is the purpose of maintaining a visited array during graph traversal? What problem occurs if it is not used?
keyPoints: Prevents processing same vertex multiple times
Avoids infinite loops in graphs with cycles
Without visited array, DFS can get stuck in cycles
Essential for correct traversal of connected/cyclic graphs

questions.question: Define connected component in an undirected graph and explain how BFS/DFS can be used to find all connected components.
keyPoints: Connected component: maximal set of vertices with paths between them
Run traversal from unvisited vertex to find one component
Repeat until all vertices visited
Count number of traversals started

questions.question: What are the time complexities of BFS and DFS? Explain why they have the same complexity.
keyPoints: Both BFS and DFS have O(V+E) time complexity
Each vertex is enqueued/visited exactly once: O(V)
Each edge is examined exactly once: O(E)
Total: O(V+E)

questions.question: Explain graph union and intersection operations with example.
keyPoints: Union: Contains all vertices and edges from both graphs
Intersection: Contains only common vertices and edges
Result may have fewer edges than sum of input graphs
These are basic operations for combining graphs

questions.question: Write an algorithm for Depth First Search (DFS) traversal. Explain how it handles cycles and visited vertices. Also analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Use stack or recursive implementation
Mark current vertex as visited, process it
For each unvisited neighbor, recursively call DFS
Handles cycles via visited array check
Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack

questions.question: Write an algorithm for Breadth First Search (BFS) traversal. Explain the use of queue in BFS and how it ensures level-order traversal. Also analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Initialize queue with source vertex, mark visited
While queue not empty: dequeue, process, enqueue unvisited neighbors
Queue ensures vertices processed in order of discovery
Level-order: all vertices at distance d processed before distance d+1
Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V) for queue and visited array

questions.question: Compare and contrast adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of graphs. For each representation, discuss: (a) Storage structure (b) Space complexity (c) Time complexity for edge lookup (d) Suitable graph type.
keyPoints: Matrix: 2D array, O(V²), O(1) edge lookup, dense graphs
List: Array of linked lists, O(V+E), O(degree) edge lookup, sparse graphs
Matrix: simple, good for complete graphs
List: efficient for real-world sparse graphs
Choice depends on graph density and operations needed

questions.question: Describe the elementary operations that can be performed on graphs. Explain how add vertex and add edge operations differ in adjacency matrix versus adjacency list representations.
keyPoints: Elementary operations: add vertex, remove vertex, add edge, remove edge, check adjacency
Matrix add vertex: O(V²) - may need to resize matrix
Matrix add edge: O(1) - simply set matrix[u][v]
List add vertex: O(1) - add new list head
List add edge: O(1) - insert at head of list
For undirected, operations performed for both directions
